title: Advanced Adjective Patterns — Become, Make, Even If, Must Be

meta.focus: Four adjective-driven patterns: ～くなる/になる (spontaneous change), ～くする/にする (deliberate change), ～くても/でも (even if), ～くなければ/でなければ (must be / if not). Plus reinforcement of ～すぎる with adjectives.

sections.title: Advanced Adjective Patterns — Become, Make, Even If, Must Be
sections.summary: You already know how adjectives describe things — hot, cool, expensive, famous. Now learn how adjectives drive change. Four patterns built on the adjective adverbial stem (く/に) let you say that things become different, that you deliberately make them different, that even if something is a certain way the outcome stands, and that something must meet a certain condition.
sections.whyItMatters: These four patterns appear constantly in daily Japanese: seasons change (涼しくなった), you make adjustments (安くしてください), you hold your position despite circumstances (むずかしくても頑張る), and you set conditions (大きくなければ入れない). Mastering them moves your Japanese from description to action.
youWillLearn: ～くなる / ～になる — express that something becomes a certain way (spontaneous change)
～くする / ～にする — express that you deliberately make something a certain way
The key contrast: なる = the subject changes by itself; する = an agent causes the change
～くても / ～でも — 'even if it is ~': concede a condition without changing the conclusion
～くなければ / ～でなければ — 'must be ~' or 'if not ~': set a minimum condition
Reinforcement of ～すぎる with adjectives (high, difficult, famous — too much)

sections.explanation: The pattern ～くなる (i-adjectives) and ～になる (na-adjectives) expresses a spontaneous or natural change of state — the subject shifts to that condition on its own, without an external agent causing it. Form the adverbial from the adjective: drop い and add く for i-adjectives; add に after the bare stem for na-adjectives. Then attach なる in the appropriate tense. This pattern pairs naturally with ～てくる (change up to now, felt by the speaker) and ～ていく (change continuing into the future).
notes: I-adjective adverbial: drop い, add く. Examples: 高い→高く、寒い→寒く、楽しい→楽しく、むずかしい→むずかしく
Na-adjective adverbial: bare stem + に. Examples: 元気→元気に、有名→有名に、大切→大切に、きれい→きれいに
Irregular: いい→よくなる (not いくなる) — the irr_ii rule applies to all adverbial derivations
With てくる: 寒くなってきた — 'It has been getting cold (and I feel it now)'. Signals gradual change toward the present.
With ていく: もっと暗くなっていく — 'It will keep getting darker (into the future)'. Signals ongoing/future progression.
Desire form: 上手になりたい (want to become skilled at ~) — 上手 is na-adj, so: 上手に + なりたい
Use ようになる (G26 pattern) for changes in ability or habit: 日本語が話せるようになった (came to be able to speak Japanese)

parts.gloss: when autumn comes (と conditional — when/if)
the air (が — subject marker)
cold-ADV (寒い → 寒く — i-adj adverbial stem)
becomes (なる — polite present)

examples.en: When autumn comes, the air becomes cold.
examples.breakdown: 寒い (i-adj) → drop い → 寒く. Attach なります (polite). The change is natural — nobody is causing the cold; it just happens.

parts.gloss: if you study every day (ば conditional)
skilled-ADV (上手 na-adj stem + に)
you can become (なれる — potential of なる, polite)

examples.en: If you study every day, you can become skilled at it.
examples.breakdown: 上手 is a na-adjective (na_adj). Adverbial: 上手 + に. Potential: なれる → なれます. The ば conditional sets the condition.

parts.gloss: this year (time context)
this town (が — subject)
famous-ADV (有名 na-adj stem + に)
has been becoming (なって + きました — change toward now, polite past)

examples.en: This town has been getting famous recently.
examples.breakdown: 有名 (na-adj) + に + なる. The てくる form (なってきました) signals gradual change that the speaker now perceives.

parts.gloss: when summer comes (と conditional)
even more (adverb)
hot-ADV (暑い → 暑く)
will keep getting (なって + いきます — ongoing future change, polite)

examples.en: When summer comes, it will keep getting hotter.
examples.breakdown: 暑い (i-adj) → 暑く. The ていく form (なっていきます) signals change that continues into the future.

sections.meaning: make ~ / cause to be ~ (deliberate change by an agent)
sections.explanation: The pattern ～くする (i-adjectives) and ～にする (na-adjectives) expresses a deliberate act of changing something to a certain state. The key difference from ～くなる is that here there is an agent who is intentionally making the change happen — the subject of する is doing it on purpose. The most common contexts are requests (もっと安くしてください), decisions (部屋を明るくした), and the fixed expression 大切にする (to treat with care / take care of).
notes: I-adjective: drop い, add く + する. Examples: 安い→安くする、明るい→明るくする、楽しい→楽しくする
Na-adjective: bare stem + にする. Examples: 元気→元気にする、大切→大切にする、きれい→きれいにする
Fixed expression: 大切にする — 'to take care of / treat with care'. 体を大切にしてください (please take care of your health).
Requests are common: もっと安くしてください (please make it cheaper)、部屋を明るくしてください (please brighten the room).
The causative ～させる is the stronger verb-based equivalent; ～にする/くする focuses on adjective-described outcomes.
Irregular: いい → よくする (not いくする)

parts.gloss: a little more (もう少し — adverb)
cheap-ADV (安い → 安く — i-adj adverbial)

parts.text: して もらえませんか。
parts.gloss: could you make it? (してもらえませんか — potential + もらう, polite request)

examples.en: Could you make it a little cheaper?
examples.breakdown: 安い (i-adj) → 安く + する. してもらえませんか is a polite way to ask someone to do something for you. The agent (the shop worker) is being asked to deliberately change the price.

parts.gloss: the room (を — object)
bright-ADV (明るい → 明るく)
I want to make it (したい + です — desire, polite)

examples.en: I want to brighten up the room.
examples.breakdown: 明るい (i-adj) → 明るく + する → したいです (desire). The speaker is the deliberate agent — they intend to act.

parts.gloss: your body/health (を — object)
carefully-ADV (大切 na-adj stem + に)
please take care (してください — te-form request)

examples.en: Please take care of your health.
examples.breakdown: 大切 (na-adj) + に + する = 大切にする (fixed expression: to treat with care). In polite request: してください.

parts.gloss: this classroom (を — object)
lively-ADV (元気 na-adj stem + に)

parts.text: して いきましょう。
parts.gloss: let's make it lively (していきましょう — ていく volitional, polite)

examples.en: Let's make this classroom a lively place going forward.
examples.breakdown: 元気 (na-adj) + に + する → していく (change continuing forward) → していきましょう (volitional polite). The ていく adds a forward-looking nuance.

sections.title: なる vs する — Spontaneous Change vs Deliberate Change

items.label: ～くなる / ～になる
points: The subject changes by itself — no agent is responsible
Seasons change, skills improve over time, situations shift on their own

parts.gloss: cold-ADV (寒い → 寒く)
became (なる — polite past)

example.en: It became cold. (The cold just happened — nobody caused it.)

items.label: ～くする / ～にする
points: An agent deliberately causes the change — someone is doing it on purpose
Making a room brighter, asking for a lower price, taking care of something
English: 'make ~ / cause ~ to be / set ~ to'

parts.gloss: the room (を — object)
bright-ADV (明るい → 明るく)
made it (する — polite past)

example.en: I made the room brighter. (I deliberately did this.)

sections.tip: Both patterns use the same adverbial stem (く for i-adj, に for na-adj). The only difference is the verb: なる = happens on its own; する = someone makes it happen.

sections.meaning: even if it is ~ / even though it is ~ (adjective concessive)
sections.explanation: The concessive pattern ～くても (i-adjectives) and ～でも (na-adjectives) means 'even if ~ / even though ~'. It concedes that a condition is true — or might be true — while insisting that the conclusion of the main clause holds regardless. This is the adjective parallel to the verb concessive ～ても (G20). For i-adjectives: drop い, add くても. For na-adjectives: stem + でも. The particle でも in this construction is different from sentence-initial でも (= 'but').
notes: I-adjective formation: drop い, add くても. 高い→高くても、むずかしい→むずかしくても、楽しい→楽しくても
Na-adjective formation: stem + でも. 有名→有名でも、元気→元気でも、大切→大切でも
Irregular: いい→よくても (not いくても)
The main clause after ～くても / ～でも often contains 〜ます / 〜ない or a plain-form verb: 行きます、頑張ります、知らない
Contrast with のに (G20) — のに expresses surprise/frustration that the main clause happened despite the condition; ～くても is neutral and predictive.
This pattern parallels verb ～ても (G20): 食べても太らない (even if I eat, I don't gain weight)

parts.gloss: the price (が — subject)
even if it's expensive (高い → 高くても — i-adj concessive)
I'll buy it (買う — polite present/future)

examples.en: Even if it's expensive, I'll buy it.
examples.breakdown: 高い (i-adj) → drop い → 高く + ても = 高くても. The speaker concedes the price is high but will buy anyway.

parts.gloss: even if it's difficult (むずかしい → むずかしくても)
I won't give up (あきらめる → あきらめません — polite negative)

examples.en: Even if it's difficult, I won't give up.
examples.breakdown: むずかしい (i-adj) → むずかしくても. The main clause is a strong negative commitment. Note: むずかしい is always written in kana (難 not in taught set).

parts.gloss: even if they're famous (有名 na-adj stem + でも)
as for me (は — topic)
I don't know (知る → 知りません — polite negative)

examples.en: Even if they're famous, I don't know them.
examples.breakdown: 有名 (na-adj) → stem + でも = 有名でも. No matter how famous, the speaker simply doesn't recognize them.

parts.gloss: even if you feel well (元気 na-adj + でも)
body (を — object)

parts.text: 大切に して ください。
parts.gloss: please take care (大切にしてください — fixed expression, polite request)

examples.en: Even if you feel well, please take care of your body.
examples.breakdown: 元気 (na-adj) + でも = 元気でも. The request stands regardless of how the person feels. 大切にする = fixed expression 'take care of'.

sections.explanation: The pattern ～くなければ (i-adjectives) and ～でなければ (na-adjectives) sets a required minimum condition, often paired with いけない or ならない to express obligation ('must be ~'), or used alone as a negative conditional ('if it is not ~'). For i-adjectives: drop い, add くなければ. For na-adjectives: stem + でなければ. This pattern parallels the verb obligation form ～なければいけない (G22) and the ば conditional (also G22).
notes: I-adjective: drop い, add くなければ. 安い→安くなければ、大きい→大きくなければ、いい→よくなければ (irregular — stem is よ-)
Na-adjective: stem + でなければ. 有名→有名でなければ、元気→元気でなければ
Paired with いけない (obligation): 安くなければいけない (must be cheap / it has to be cheap)
Paired with ならない (stricter/written): 元気でなければならない (must be healthy — more formal tone)
Standalone conditional: 有名でなければ、知らない — 'if they're not famous, I won't know them'
Casual spoken contraction: ～くなきゃ / ～でなきゃ — 安くなきゃ (if it's not cheap, I won't [buy it])
This pattern is the adjective equivalent of verb 〜なければいけない (taught in G22)

parts.gloss: the price (が — subject)
if it's not cheap (安い → 安くなければ — i-adj conditional negative)
I won't buy it (買う → 買いません — polite negative)

examples.en: If it's not cheap, I won't buy it.
examples.breakdown: 安い (i-adj) → drop い → 安く + なければ = 安くなければ. The condition: price must be cheap or the speaker won't buy.

parts.gloss: this door (は — topic)
if it's not big (大きい → 大きくなければ)
it must (いけない — obligation, polite)

examples.en: This door has to be big (it must be big).
examples.breakdown: 大きくなければいけません = must be big. いけない adds the obligation meaning. Literally: 'if not big, it won't do'.

parts.gloss: if not famous (有名 na-adj + でなければ — negative conditional)
at this event (に+は — location + topic)
cannot enter (入る → potential negative, polite)

examples.en: If you're not famous, you can't get into this event.
examples.breakdown: 有名 (na-adj) + でなければ = 有名でなければ. The condition: must be famous to enter.

parts.gloss: the body (が — subject)
if not healthy (元気 na-adj + でなければ)
nothing / anything (何も — total negative)
cannot do (できる → polite negative)

examples.en: If you're not healthy, you can't do anything.
examples.breakdown: 元気 (na-adj) + でなければ = 元気でなければ. Strong negative conditional emphasizing health as a prerequisite for everything.

sections.title: Adjective Pattern Paradigm — All Four Patterns
sections.description: Summary of how i-adjectives and na-adjectives form all four patterns taught in this lesson, plus the reinforcement of ～すぎる. Use this as a reference chart.

rows.label: Adverbial (base stem)
cells: ADV base — used in all four patterns below

rows.label: ～くなってきた
cells: has been getting expensive / famous (change toward now)

rows.label: ～くなっていく
cells: will keep getting expensive / famous (continuing future)

rows.label: ～くなりたい / ～になりたい
cells: want to get better / want to become famous
rows.meaning: desire to change (adj + なりたい)

rows.label: ～くする / ～にする
cells: make it expensive / make it famous

rows.label: ～くしてください
cells: きれいにしてください
please make it cheap / please make it clean

rows.label: ～くても / ～でも
cells: even if it's expensive / even if they're famous

rows.label: ～くなければ / ～でなければ
cells: if not cheap / if not healthy

rows.label: ～すぎる (G25 reinforcement)
rows.meaning: excessive degree — uses adj stem (drop い for i-adj)

notes: Irregular いい: adverbial = よく. All patterns follow: よくなる、よくする、よくても、よくなければ
～すぎる for adjectives: i-adj drops い before すぎる (高すぎる). Na-adj uses bare stem (有名すぎる). Both use sugiru_form.
The adverbial stem (く/に) is the foundation — all four patterns add different endings to the same base.
大切にする is a fixed expression: 'to take care of / treat with care' — not simply 'make it important'

parts.gloss: when autumn comes (と conditional)
gradually (adverb)
cold-ADV (寒い → 寒く — i-adj adverbial)
is getting (なる + てくる — gradual change toward now, polite)

examples.en: When autumn comes, it gradually gets cold.
examples.note: 寒い (i-adj) → 寒く + なる. The てくる form signals a gradual change the speaker perceives building toward them.

examples.context: Making a request — くする (deliberate change)

parts.gloss: this room (を — object)
even more (もっと)
bright-ADV (明るい → 明るく)

parts.text: して もらえませんか。
parts.gloss: could you make it? (してもらえませんか — potential of もらう, polite request)

examples.en: Could you make this room a little brighter?
examples.note: 明るい (i-adj) → 明るく + する. The speaker is asking someone else to act deliberately. Contrast with なる — the room won't brighten by itself.

parts.gloss: the exam (が — subject)
even if it's difficult (むずかしい → むずかしくても)
without giving up (あきらめる → negative te-form あきらめず + に)
I'll do my best (頑張る → 頑張ります — polite)

examples.en: Even if the exam is difficult, I'll do my best without giving up.
examples.note: むずかしい (i-adj) → むずかしくても. The concession doesn't change the resolve. Always write むずかしい in kana (難 not in taught kanji set).

examples.context: Setting a condition — くなければ (must be)

parts.gloss: this job (は — topic)
body/health (が — subject)
if not healthy (元気 na-adj + でなければ — negative conditional)
cannot do it (できる → polite negative)

examples.en: For this job, you cannot do it if you're not healthy.
examples.note: 元気 (na-adj) + でなければ = 元気でなければ. The condition is explicit: health is required. でなければ sets the threshold.

parts.gloss: this exam (は — topic)
too difficult (むずかしい → むずかしすぎる → te-form)
nothing (何も — total negation)
I couldn't write anything (書く → potential negative, polite past)

examples.en: The exam was too difficult, and I couldn't write anything.
examples.note: i-adj すぎる: drop い → むずかしすぎる. Then te-form: むずかしすぎて (connector). G25 pattern reinforced here.

examples.context: Reinforcement: になれる (potential of なる) with たら conditional (G22) and と思います (G27)

parts.gloss: after studying every day (たら conditional — when/after)
more skillfully (上手 na-adj + に)
if you can become (なれる + と conditional — natural result)
I think (と思う — G27 reinforcement, polite)

examples.en: I think if you practise every day, you'll be able to become more skilled.
examples.note: 上手になれる = potential of 上手になる. Combined with と思います (G27). The conditional たら shows the path to change.

sections.title: Conversation 1: The Seasons Are Changing
sections.context: Rikizo asks Yamamoto-sensei about the weather and then about studying. Polite register.

lines.jp: やまもと先生、今年の 秋は すずしく なってきましたね。
lines.en: Yamamoto-sensei, it's been getting cool this autumn, hasn't it?

lines.jp: そうですね。秋に なると、空気が すずしく なります。もうすぐ 寒くなるでしょう。
lines.en: That's right. When autumn comes, the air gets cool. It'll probably get cold soon.

lines.jp: そうですね。先生、私は 日本語が もっと 上手に なりたいんですが、どうすれば いいですか。
lines.en: That's right. Sensei, I want to become better at Japanese — what should I do?

lines.jp: むずかしくても、毎日 勉強する ことが 大切ですよ。毎日 勉強 すれば、上手に なれますよ。
lines.en: Even if it's difficult, studying every day is important. If you study every day, you'll be able to become skilled.

lines.jp: 分かりました。毎日 勉強 するように します。ありがとうございます。
lines.en: I understand. I'll make sure to study every day. Thank you.

lines.jp: 元気に なるように、体も 大切に して くださいね。
lines.en: Please take care of your health too, so you can stay well.

sections.title: Conversation 2: Exam Prep (Casual)
sections.context: Two college friends, Taro and Rikizo, talk about an upcoming exam. Casual register.

lines.jp: ねえ、今度の 試験は むずかしいって 聞いたよ。
lines.en: Hey, I heard this exam is difficult.

lines.jp: うん、でも むずかしくても、毎日 勉強 している よ。
lines.en: Yeah, but even if it's hard, I study every day.

lines.jp: すごいね。試験は むずかしくなければ いいけど、私も 早く 始めないと な。
lines.en: That's amazing. I hope the exam isn't too hard, but I'd better start early too.

lines.jp: たろうは 日本語が 上手 だから、だいじょうぶ だ と 思うよ。でも 試験が 近くなって きたし、早く 始めた 方が いいよ。
lines.en: Taro, you're good at Japanese, so I think you'll be fine. But the exam is getting close, so it's better to start early.

lines.jp: そうだ ね。試験が 終わったら、いっしょに ご飯 食べに 行こうか。
lines.en: You're right. When the exam is over, shall we go eat together?

lines.jp: うん、もちろん 行こう！試験が 終わったら、たくさん 食べたいな。
lines.en: Yeah, of course! When the exam's done, I want to eat a lot.

sections.title: Adjective Pattern Drill
sections.instructions: Change the adjective in brackets to the pattern shown. Type the full result.

items.hint: i-adj: drop い, add く + なります

items.targetForm: ～くする (て-form request: ～くしてください)
items.hint: i-adj: drop い, add く + してください

items.targetForm: ～にする (te-form request: ～にしてください)
items.answerReading: げんきにしてください
items.hint: na-adj: stem + にしてください
choices: 元気になってください

items.hint: i-adj: drop い, add くても
i-adj: drop い, add くなければ
i-adj: drop い, add すぎる (no く needed for すぎる)

sections.title: Fill in the Blank
sections.instructions: Choose the correct adjective form to complete each sentence.

items.before: 毎日 勉強したら、日本語が もっと 上手
items.translation: I think if you study every day, you'll become more skilled at Japanese.
items.explanation: 上手 is a na-adjective. The pattern is 上手 + に + なる. The と conditional here means 'if/when'. 上手くなる is incorrect — 上手 takes に, not く.

items.before: この 教室を もっと 明る
items.translation: Could you make this classroom brighter?
items.explanation: 明るい is an i-adjective. ～くする (deliberate change) → て-form: くして. The speaker is asking someone to act. ～になる is spontaneous, not deliberate.

items.translation: Even if this book is expensive, I'll buy it.
items.explanation: 高い is an i-adjective. Concessive form: drop い, add くても. でも would be used for na-adjectives (e.g. 有名でも).

items.after: 、このイベントには 入れません。
items.translation: If you're not famous, you can't get into this event.
items.explanation: 有名 is a na-adjective. Negative conditional: stem + でなければ. くなければ is for i-adjectives. でも would be concessive (even if), not a negative condition.

items.before: この 仕事は、体が 元気
items.translation: If you're not healthy, you cannot do this job.
items.explanation: 元気 is a na-adjective. Negative conditional: でなければ. 元気くなければ is incorrect — 元気 does not take く.

items.before: この 映画は 長す
items.after: 、見るのが むずかしいです。
items.translation: This movie is too long, and it's hard to watch.
items.explanation: 長い (i-adj) → drop い → 長す + ぎる = 長すぎる (too long). Te-form: 長すぎて (connector). G25 reinforcement.

sections.title: Mixed Practice
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.question: Which sentence means 'It got cool' (natural change, no one caused it)?
choices: すずしくてもいいです。
すずしくなければいけません。
items.explanation: ～くなる expresses spontaneous/natural change. ～くする would mean 'I made it cool' (deliberate). ～くても = even if. ～くなければ = must be / if not.

items.question: The teacher asked the student to make the classroom brighter. Which sentence fits?
choices: 教室が 明るくなりました。
教室を 明るくしてください。
教室が 明るくても。
教室が 明るくなければ。
items.answer: 教室を 明るくしてください。
items.explanation: ～くしてください = 'please make it ~' (deliberate change by the listener). ～くなりました = it became (spontaneous). The を marks the thing being changed.

items.question: りきぞ said: 「むずかしくても、____ます。」 Which ending makes the sentence mean 'Even if it's difficult, I'll do it'?
items.explanation: The main clause after ～くても should express the conclusion. 'I'll do it' → します (polite). します → し (ます-stem before ます). The stem + ます = polite present affirmative.

items.question: Which sentence means 'You must be healthy to do this job' (na-adjective obligation)?
choices: この 仕事は 元気くなければ いけません。
この 仕事は 元気でなければ いけません。
この 仕事は 元気になければ いけません。
この 仕事は 元気もなければ いけません。
items.answer: この 仕事は 元気でなければ いけません。
items.explanation: 元気 is a na-adjective. Negative conditional: stem + でなければ. 元気くなければ is wrong (くなければ is for i-adj). 元気になければ is also incorrect — the pattern is でなければ.

items.question: What is the difference between 教室が 明るくなった and 教室を 明るくした?
choices: Both mean the classroom became bright naturally.
なった = someone made it bright; した = it became bright on its own.
なった = it became bright on its own; した = someone deliberately made it bright.
There is no difference — both are interchangeable.
items.answer: なった = it became bright on its own; した = someone deliberately made it bright.
items.explanation: なる = spontaneous change (the subject changes by itself). する = deliberate change (an agent causes it). が marks the subject (classroom changes on its own); を marks the object of a deliberate action.

items.question: Which sentence best fits: 'Even if the book is expensive, I want to read it'?
choices: 本が 高くなると、読みたいです。
本が 高くなければ、読みたいです。
本が 高くても、読みたいです。
本が 高くして、読みたいです。
items.answer: 本が 高くても、読みたいです。
items.explanation: ～くても = 'even if it is ~ (i-adj)'. The speaker concedes the high price but still wants to read. 高くなると = if/when it gets expensive (conditional). 高くなければ = if it's not expensive.

items.question: りきぞ said he wants to become skilled at Japanese. How would he say: 'I want to become good at Japanese'?
choices: 日本語が 上手くなりたいです。
日本語が 上手になりたいです。
日本語を 上手にしたいです。
日本語が 上手でなければ なりません。
items.answer: 日本語が 上手になりたいです。
items.explanation: 上手 is a na-adjective. Pattern: stem + になりたい. 上手くなりたい is incorrect — 上手 takes に not く. 上手にしたい would mean 'I want to make Japanese good' (deliberately changing it).
